coredata.dc:title: Nafion coated Au nanoparticle-graphene quantum dot nanocomposite modified working electrode for voltammetric determination of dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Jang, Hong-Sik
Kim, Dongwon
Lee, Chaedong
Yan, Bingyi
Qin, Xinyu
Piao, Yuanzhe
coredata.dc:description: In this work, glassy carbon electrode was modified by Nafion coated gold nanoparticle-graphene quantum dots (Au-GQDs-Nafion) nanocomposite and used for voltammetric determination of trace dopamine. The properties of the as-prepared material were characterized by UV–vis spectrometry, fluorescence spectrometry and TEM. The Au-GQDs-Nafion composite showed the excellent synergistic effect for the electrochemical detection of dopamine (DA). Under optimum conditions, the differential voltammetric (DPV) peak current and the concentration of DA displayed a linear range of 2 μM to 50 μM with the detection limit of 0.84 μM. Furthermore, the as-prepared electrode also showed selectivity toward DA detection in the existence of uric acid (UA) and ascorbic acid (AA). The interference experiment showed, that most of the ions did not show obvious influence on the detection of DA. The modified electrode presented reasonable stability and reproducibility in the voltammetric determination of DA, and also the nanocomposite was applied for the real sample analysis in human urine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Au nanoparticles
Graphene quantum dots
Dopamine
Voltammetric determination
Real analysis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic preparation of Au-GQDs-Nafion/GCE and voltammetric determination of dopamine.

(A) Visual images of GQDs, Au-GQDs nanocomposite and AuNPs (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of each material. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of each material. (D) TEM image of Au-GQDs nanocomposite. (E) Size distribution histogram of Au-GQDs. (F) Representative HR-TEM image of as-prepared GQDs.
CV responses of 0.2 mM (DA) on the bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, AuNPs/GCE, Au-GQDs/GCE and Au-GQDs-Nafion/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) with the scan rate of 100 mV s−1.
(A) CV responses of 0.2 mM (DA) on the Au-GQDs-Nafion/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) with different scan rates. (B) A Linear relationship between redox peak currents and scan rate.

CV responses of 0.2 mM (DA) on the Au-GQDs-Nafion/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (different pH from 4.0 to 8.0) with the scan rate of 100 mV s−1. (B) Plots of currents (I pa) and oxidation potential (E pa) versus pH.
Plots of the peak current of 0.2 mM DA versus the accumulation time from 30 s to 3 min in PBS (pH 7.4) at the Au-GQDs-Nafion/GCE by DPV method.
(A) DPV responses of Au-GQDs-Nafion/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) with different DA concentrations from 2 μM to 50 μM. (B) The linear plot for oxidation peak current (I pa) and concentration of DA.
DPV responses of 50 μM DA with interference materials: (A) 100 μM AA (ascorbic acid) and 30 μM UA (uric acid). (B) 50 μM Glu (glucose) and 100 μM Na2SO4. (C) 200 μM NaCl and KCl.

(A) Reproducibility for DA oxidation with six independent Au-GQDs-Nafion/GCEs in solution containing 0.2 mM DA, 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) with the scan rate of 100 mV s−1 (CV method). (B) Stability for DA oxidation with Au-GQDs-Nafion modified electrode for 5 days after storage (DPV method).
Comparison of different electrochemical sensors proposed for determination of DA.
Detection result of DA in human urine samples by using Au-GQDs-Nafion modified electrode (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Nafion coated Au nanoparticle-graphene quantum dot nanocomposite modified working electrode for voltammetric determination of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School of Convergence Science and Technology, Seoul National University, Seoul 151-742, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate School of Convergence Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Graduate School of Convergence Science and Technology, Seoul National University, Seoul 151-742, Republic of Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate School of Convergence Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, glassy carbon electrode was modified by Nafion coated gold nanoparticle-graphene quantum dots (Au-GQDs-Nafion) nanocomposite and used for voltammetric determination of trace dopamine. The properties of the as-prepared material were characterized by UV–vis spectrometry, fluorescence spectrometry and TEM. The Au-GQDs-Nafion composite showed the excellent synergistic effect for the electrochemical detection of dopamine (DA). Under optimum conditions, the differential voltammetric (DPV) peak current and the concentration of DA displayed a linear range of 2 μM to 50 μM with the detection limit of 0.84 μM. Furthermore, the as-prepared electrode also showed selectivity toward DA detection in the existence of uric acid (UA) and ascorbic acid (AA). The interference experiment showed, that most of the ions did not show obvious influence on the detection of DA. The modified electrode presented reasonable stability and reproducibility in the voltammetric determination of DA, and also the nanocomposite was applied for the real sample analysis in human urine.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) works as an important neurotransmitter in the central nervous system and contributes significantly to the neurological control of emotion, movement, perception, and motivation [1]. In biological environments, transfer of signals to the brain and other organs is usually regulated by DA [2]. However, an abnormal level of DA has been implicated in neurological disorders such as schizophrenia, Parkinson's and Huntington's diseases [3–5]. Thus, it is quite essential to quantify and detect the concentration of dopamine under biological conditions. In the literature, Dopamine (DA) has been monitored by chromatography-mass spectroscopy [6,7] and fluorescence methods [8]. Compared to these described methods, electrochemical methods have drawn great interest due to their beneficial characteristics of simplicity, rapid response, cost-effectiveness and high sensitivity [9,10].

Generally, conventional electrochemical methods were widely used for the determination of DA. However, the electron-transfer was sluggish and also poor response signals were observed at the bare glassy carbon electrode [11,12]. Moreover, DA (dopamine), AA (Ascorbic acid) and UA (Uric acid) show overlapping of the oxidation peaks at conventional electrodes, which makes it difficult to selectively detect DA [13–16].

To fix these problems, graphene-based materials, such as graphene [17], graphene-gold [18], graphene-tin oxide [19], and graphene-chitosan [20], have been extensively used to modify the working electrode for the selective determination of DA. The reason is that π-π interaction between the phenyl moiety of DA and π-conjugated graphene structure enables the electron transfer [21–23]. These materials contribute to the selective determination of dopamine. Nevertheless, they exhibit limited detection sensitivity and also involve complicated synthetic procedures.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterial with a plane size less than 100 nm and a thickness less than 10 layers [24], have aroused increasing attention in biomedical fields because of small size, chemical inertness, stable photoluminescence (PL), low toxicity and biocompatibility [25]. Furthermore, GQDs have unique properties, including high surface area, conductivity, and good dispersibility, which enables the promising applications of GQDs in energy-related applications [26–29]. As a new member of graphene, GQDs have advantageous potentials for the electrochemical detection. Especially, Pang et al. employed GQD as a selective electrochemical detection of dopamine, because GQDs have negative carboxyl groups, which not only offered good stability but also could electrostatically interact with amine groups in dopamine. Moreover, π-π stacking between GQDs and dopamine makes electron transfer and communication stronger [30].

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are excellent electrocatalytic materials for the electrochemical determination toward various materials due to superior conductivity, high specific surface area, and great catalytic properties [31]. They play a pivotal role in enhancing the electron transfer capability [32] and improving voltammetric detection performance [33]. The synergistic cooperation of AuNP and GQDs can offer selective and sensitive environments for the electrochemical sensing of numerous substances [32–35]. Nafion is a cation exchange polymer, which has been widely used as a membrane on electrode surfaces. The membrane has the ability to attract cations and preclude the approach of anionic species [36]. Nafion's ion-permeability for cationic species is especially crucial in the selective detection of DA because it can remove the interference effects caused by anionic ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA) [37]. In addition to this, Nafion was employed as a coating material [37] to make Au-GQDs nanocomposite firmly attached to the electrode surface, which results in the significantly improved stability and reproducibility [30].

In this work, the Au-GQDs composite was produced simply via a facile one-pot reaction by using GQDs, gold ions (AuCl4 −) and sodium citrate as reducing agents [38]. The Au-GQDs-Nafion modified electrode was prepared by pipetting the Au-GQDs composite onto the pretreated GCE surface and dried in air, followed by adding diluted Nafion onto the Au-GQDs modified electrode surface. It is anticipated that the Au-GQDs-Nafion/GCE exhibits synergistic cooperation toward the voltammetric sensing of DA, and the modified electrode shows lower detection limits than the methods reported in the selective detection of DA. Furthermore, it also revealed good stability, reproducibility and potential application in the real sample analysis.

Vulcan CX-72 carbon black was obtained from Cabot Corporation. Gold (III) chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), Dopamine, tri-sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7) and Nafion (5%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. A standard stock solution of dopamine was produced by dissolving dopamine in distilled water and stored in the refrigerator (4 °C). The supporting electrolyte was phosphate buffer solution (PBS 0.1 M) of different pH values. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification.

The optical absorption spectrum data were collected from a Lambda 35 UV–vis spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded by using Fluorescence spectrometer-2 (Scinco, Korea). The morphology of the Au-GQDs nanocomposite was observed by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-2010). Electrochemical measurements were carried out on a conventional 3-electrode system with an AUTO-LAB potentiostat (Metrohm, USA). The 3-electrode system was composed of the modified glassy carbon electrode as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and the platinum wire as the counter electrode.

GQDs were synthesized according to the previous work with slight modification [39]. Specifically, 0.2 g of dried CX-72 carbon black was put into 50 ml of 6 mol L−1 HNO3, followed by refluxing for 24 h. The suspension was cooled down to room temperature and centrifuged for 10 min. While the sediment was discarded, the obtained supernatant was dialyzed (3500 Da, Dialysis bag). After dialysis, the obtained solution (9 ml) was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and hydrothermally treated by using the ammonia solution (1 ml) at 180 °C for 12 h with some alteration from the previous research [40]. After cooling down to room temperature, the obtained solution was once again dialyzed (3500 Da, Dialysis bag). Preparation of Au-GQDs nanocomposite was based on the previous paper with some modifications [35]. Briefly, the prepared GQDs solution (1 ml) was added to the 4 ml of distilled water. 400 μl (25.4 mM) of the HAuCl4 solution was put into the 5 ml of GQDs solution, and the mixture was heated and vigorously stirred for 15 min. 400 μl (0.1 M) of trisodium citrate solution was quickly added to the heated solution. The solution underwent a reflux process for 30 min until its color changed into a purplish red. Finally, the synthesized composite was allowed to cool and stay at room temperature.

AuNPs were prepared according to previous research with some modifications [35]. Briefly, 400 μl (25.4 mM) of the HAuCl4 solution was put into the boiling distilled water (5 ml) that was vigorously stirred, and then 400 μl (0.1 M) of trisodium citrate solution was quickly dropped into the heated solution until it attained a wine red color. Finally, the obtained AuNPs were allowed to cool and stored at room temperature.

In the first place, Nafion (5 wt%) was diluted to the 1 wt% of Nafion with ethanol. Before the modification of electrodes, the bare GCE was polished by 0.3 μm alumina slurries on a felt pad, cleaned with water and ethanol for 10 min and then dried under the infrared lamp. The procedure for preparing the modified electrodes was outlined in Fig. 1 . 5 μl of Au-GQDs nanocomposite was drop-cast onto the bare GCE and dried by the infrared lamp. After that, 1 μl of diluted Nafion (1 wt%) was added onto the Au-GQDs/GCE surface and dried under the infrared lamp to prepare for the voltammetric sensing of dopamine.

A 20 ml volume of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) and a certain amount of dopamine or sample solution was put into the electrochemical cell, and subsequently, the three-electrode system was arranged into it. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was carried out in the potential range from 0 to 0.6 V with the scan rate of 100 mV s−1. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was conducted in the potential range from 0 to 0.6 V with the following conditions: the pulse amplitude of 4 mV and a pulse width of 2 s.

Fig. 2A displays the visual images of each material. The GQDs solution (left) is transparent and reveals a light blond color. And the Au-GQDs solution (middle) exhibits a purplish red color. At the same time, the AuNPs solution (right) shows a wine red color. The optical properties of as-synthesized materials were studied by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption spectra of each material were shown in the Fig. 2B. GQDs have broad absorption spectra below 800 nm. The successful synthesis of AuNPs was verified by the absorption peak around 520 nm [35,41]. Furthermore, compared with the pure AuNPs, the Au-GQDs nanocomposite exhibits a red-shifted and broadened absorption peak, which arises from the increase in Au particle size [35,42] through the neutralization of positively charged AuNPs and negatively charged GQDs, during which forms strong interaction between these two kinds of nanoparticles [35]. The fluorescence emission spectra of each material were described in the Fig. 2C. With the excitation energy at 466 nm, GQDs present the peak emission intensity (I0 = 2771.96 a.u.) at 514.8 nm. However, the Au-GQDs nanocomposites have the peak emission intensity (I0 = 113.65 a.u.) at 514.8 nm. The huge quench of fluorescence is caused by the successful fabrication of Au-GQDs nanocomposites. The mechanism was explained by Y. Liu et al. that AuNPs can quench the fluorescent GQDs by two ways: (1) making a composite with GQDs and (2) quenching indirectly at some distance [42]. Our case belongs to the first circumstance. Fig. 2D shows the TEM image of Au-GQDs nanocomposites, which indicates that part of GQDs attached on the AuNPs surface and the rest remained in the solution. From the size distribution histogram in Fig. 2E obtained from TEM, we can identify that AuNPs possessed the diameter of 12.8 ± 1.2 nm. Fig. 2F revealed the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of as-prepared GQDs, which informs the synthesis of quantum sized carbon.

Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) is a useful method to investigate the electron transfer rate and the interaction between different analytes and the electrode surface [43]. The CV curves of DA (0.2 mM) on different electrodes in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7.4) are presented in Fig. 3 . Poor redox peaks were observed on bare GCE, indicating a slow electron transfer process; however, Au-GQDs-Nafion/GCE showed a higher oxidation peak current in comparison to GQDs/GCE, AuNP/GCE and Au-GQDs/GCE. This phenomenon may be ascribed to the synergistic cooperation of three components. Au nanoparticles are electrocatalytic materials, which can improve the electron transfer capability and further enhance the electrochemical performance. Additionally, GQDs have many carboxyl groups, which not only provides a good material stability, but also enables the interaction with DA through π-π and electrostatic forces [44]. Nafion is the anchoring agent, which makes Au-GQDs composite firmly attached to the working electrode and accordingly improves the reproducibility and stability [30]. Also, it could prevent electronegative AA and UA from approaching working electrode to improve the selectivity of DA [45].

Fig. 4 reveals the CV responses of 0.2 mM DA at Au-GQDs-Nafion/GCE with different scan rates from 50 to 350 mV s−1. With the increase of scan rates, redox peak currents increase at the same time. Also, the linear relationship between the redox peak current of DA and scan rate can be obtained, which indicates the typical adsorption-controlled process [46,47].

The linear regression equation is I pa (μA) = 0.04426 v (V s−1) + 2.263 (R2 = 0.994), and I pc (μA) = −0.0456 v (V s−1) − 0.0467 (R2 = 0.997), respectively. In addition, anodic peak potential slightly moved toward the positive side and cathodic peak potential slightly shifted toward the negative side with increased scan rate.

The influence of pH on the electrochemical redox behaviors of DA was investigated with different pH values (range from 4 to 8) in Fig. 5 . The oxidation peak potential shifted negatively with the increase of pH value, meaning that protons are participating in the electrochemical reaction [48]. The linear relationship between the E pa and pH was obtained from the linear regression equation as E pa (V) = −0.0418 pH + 0. 5769 (R2 = 0.994) with a slope of 41.8 mV/pH. Although the clarification of the mechanism on the working electrode has been beyond the main subject of this paper, the oxidation mechanism is believed to take place via with two protons (2H+) and two electrons (2e−) transfer process to form a dopamine-o-quinone [49,50]. The oxidation peak current of DA continuously decreases from the pH 4 to pH 8. However, pH 7.4 is selected as the optimal value for the electrochemical detection of DA, because its value is close to normal physiological conditions [51].

It is obvious that the accumulation time will influence the sensitivity of the prepared sensor. The accumulation procedure can multiply the quantity of DA adsorbed on the modified electrode surfaces and subsequently improve the response signal toward DA [46,52]. The influence of accumulation time was studied from the 30 s to 3 min with DPV. Fig. 6 revealed that the current response increased from the 30 s to 1 min. However, when the accumulation time reached 2 min, the response current was decreased slightly due to the saturation of DA at the modified electrode. Accordingly, 1 min was selected as the optimal accumulation time.

The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) method was conducted for the voltammetric sensing of DA because of its quantitative analysis ability and higher sensitivity comparing with CV method [53,54]. Also, it can eliminate the contribution of non-faradaic currents [55]. Under selected conditions, the oxidation peak current of DA was proportional to its concentration, ranging from 2 μM to 50 μM (Fig. 7A). The linear regression equation can be obtained as I pa (μA) = 0.04745 + 0.06043C (μM) with the correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.998). The detection limit was 0.84 μM. Furthermore, the electrochemical sensor proposed in this work showed the lower detection limit and better electrochemical detection performance than the other methods reported in Table 1 . However, upon increasing the DA concentration over 50 μM (Not shown here), it was found that current sensitivity was rather decreased and consequently led to a lower response, which may be due to the limited kinetics from modified electrode material. Hence only the narrow detection range from 2 μM to 50 μM was considered in this DPV result.

Fig. 8 shows the influence of 100 μM ascorbic acid (AA), 30 μM uric acid (UA), 50 μM glucose (Glu), 200 μM NaCl and KCl,100 μM Na2SO4 on the detection of 50 μM DA. The DPV signal does not change significantly when the interfering substances are added. At biological conditions (pH 7.4), AA (pKa = 4.10) and UA (pKa = 5.27) exist in the anionic form, while DA (pKa = 8.9) exists in the cationic form [62,63]. For this reason, Nafion membrane has excellent ion-permeable abilities to attract strongly cationic DA and also highly repel anionic AA and UA to reach the electrode, which could lead to the selective voltammetric detection of DA [64]. Additionally, the inside Au-GQDs possessing abundant carboxyl groups which are negatively charged could further prevent the approaching of these two interfering substances in a certain way. Reproducibility and stability are essential factors for the electrochemical sensing [64]. Reproducibility test was carried out on six modified electrodes by CV method (Fig. 9A), which showed a good reproducibility with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.8%. The stability test was performed over 5 days by DPV method (Fig. 9B). No conspicuous change was noticed and it maintained 92% of its initial current after storage of 5 days, indicating good stability of the DA sensor.

The Au-GQDs-Nafion nanocomposite was utilized for the detection of DA in human urine samples. The urine samples (40 μM) were diluted to 20 ml with PBS pH 7.4 before the measurements. The results in Table 2 indicated that the recoveries of the spiked samples were in the range from 95% to 98%. Thus, the proposed method may have the potential application for DA sensing in real sample analysis.

In this work, the glassy carbon electrode was modified with Nafion coated gold nanoparticle-graphene quantum dots (Au-GQDs-Nafion) nanocomposite, which was employed for the voltammetric sensing of dopamine. It showed excellent synergistic cooperation and significantly enhanced peak current toward the electrochemical detection of dopamine (DA) compared with GQD/GCE, AuNP/GCE and Au-GQD/GCE. Under optimal conditions, the differential pulse voltammetric (DPV) peak current and the concentration of DA displayed a linear range from 2 μM to 50 μM with the detection limit of 0.84 μM. In addition, this proposed sensor presented the selective determination of DA in the presence of some interfering substances. The modified electrode displayed reasonable stability and reproducibility in voltammetric sensing of DA, and was also applied to human urine samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2015R1D1A1A01060398). This work was also supported by the Centre for Integrated Smart Sensors funded by the Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning, Republic of Korea, as Global Frontier Project (CISS-012M3A6A6054186).

Vilanova, M.A. Sogorb, A simple and rapid HPLC-MS method for the simultaneous determination of epinephrine, norepinephrine, dopamine and 5-hydroxytryptamine: application to the secretion of bovine chromaffin cell cultures, J. Tagarelli, Development of a simple and rapid solid phase microextraction-gas chromatography-triple quadrupole mass spectrometry method for the analysis of dopamine, serotonin and norepinephrine in human urine, Anal. Chim. Acta, 810 (2014) 17-24.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 3-Aminophenyl boronic acid-functionalized CuInS2 quantum dots as a near-infrared fluorescence probe for the determination of dopamine

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene and its nanocomposite material based electrochemical sensor platform for dopamine

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modification of carbon aerogel electrode with molecularly imprinted polypyrrole for electrochemical determination of dopamine

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The determination of dopamine using glassy carbon electrode pretreated by a simple electrochemical method

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molybdenum disulfide nanosheet decorated with silver nanoparticles for selective detection of dopamine

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensitive electrochemical sensors for simultaneous determination of ascorbic acid, dopamine, and uric acid based on Au@Pd-reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous determination of ascorbic acid, dopamine and uric acid with chitosan-graphene modified electrode
